questions.stem: A previously healthy 15-month-old comes in for routine preventive care in Canada. Which standardized record is the appropriate framework to structure this visit?
questions.explain: The Rourke Baby Record is the Canadian standard for infants and young children. The Greig Health Record takes over for ages 6–17.

questions.stem: A tired parent of a 5-week-old says she sometimes falls asleep feeding the baby on the living-room couch, and that the crib has a soft bumper and a small pillow "so he's comfortable." What is the most appropriate counselling?
options: Couch co-sleeping is fine as long as she stays close to the infant
Place the infant on his back on a firm flat surface, alone, with no soft bedding, pillows, or bumpers, and avoid couch co-sleeping
Have the infant sleep prone to reduce reflux and choking
Add a weighted blanket to help him sleep through the night
questions.explain: Safe sleep: back to sleep, firm flat surface, no soft bedding/pillows/bumpers/toys, avoid co-sleeping (couch/sofa sleeping is especially dangerous), avoid smoke exposure. This reduces SIDS and asphyxiation.

questions.stem: A 12-month-old is due for MMR and varicella. He has clear rhinorrhea and a temperature of 37.9°C, feeding well and playful. His mother is breastfeeding and worried the vaccines aren't safe today. What is the best approach?
options: Defer all vaccines until he is completely well
Withhold the live vaccines because he is breastfed
Proceed with vaccination today — mild illness and breastfeeding are not contraindications
Give only inactivated vaccines and reschedule the live ones
questions.explain: Mild illness, low-grade fever, and breastfeeding are not contraindications. True reasons to withhold live vaccines are severe immunocompromise and pregnancy. Deferring needlessly leaves the infant unprotected.

questions.stem: During a 2-month immunization visit, which combination most effectively reduces the infant's procedural pain?
options: Restraining the infant flat and giving the most painful vaccine first
Breastfeeding or oral sucrose, upright parental holding, distraction, and giving the most painful vaccine last
Applying ice to the leg for 20 minutes beforehand
questions.explain: Evidence-based pain reduction: breast/bottle feeding or oral sucrose/dextrose, upright cuddling, distraction, topical anesthetic when appropriate, and give the most painful vaccine last.

questions.stem: A 15-year-old presents with her father for a routine visit. What is the most appropriate way to conduct the psychosocial assessment?
options: Keep the father present throughout and promise the teen absolute confidentiality
Spend part of the visit alone with the teen, explain confidentiality and its limits, then complete a HEEADSSS assessment
Skip psychosocial screening unless she raises a concern
Defer all sensitive topics until age 18
questions.explain: Adolescent care needs private time and an explanation of confidentiality and its limits (harm to self/others, abuse, inability to consent) before a HEEADSSS history. Absolute confidentiality cannot be promised when safety is at risk.

questions.stem: A girl is brought in for short stature. Her mother is 160 cm and her father is 178 cm. Using the mid-parental height method, what is her target (mid-parental) height?
questions.explain: Girl = [maternal + (paternal − 13)] / 2 = [160 + (178 − 13)] / 2 = [160 + 165] / 2 = 162.5 cm. (The boy formula instead adds 13 to the maternal height.)

questions.stem: A 13-year-old boy is the shortest in his class and has not started puberty. His growth velocity is normal, bone age is delayed by ~2 years, and his father reports being a "late bloomer" who grew until age 19. Exam and proportions are normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Pathologic short stature requiring extensive endocrine workup
Constitutional delay of growth and puberty
questions.explain: Delayed bone age + normal growth velocity + family history of late bloomers = constitutional delay. Final adult height is usually normal; it is more common in boys.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old previously tracking along the 25th percentile has dropped to below the 3rd percentile over 18 months, growing only ~2 cm in the past year. Which single feature most strongly indicates this is pathologic rather than a normal variant?
options: Her parents are both below average height
Her bone age is mildly delayed
Her poor growth velocity with percentile crossing
She has not yet entered puberty
questions.explain: Crossing percentiles with poor growth velocity is the red flag — short stature with poor velocity is pathologic until proven otherwise. A single low point or short parents (familial pattern) is far less concerning than a falling trajectory.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old girl's parents ask what the first physical sign of puberty will be. What should you tell them?
questions.explain: In girls, puberty (begins ~8–13 y) typically starts with breast development, then pubic hair, then the growth spurt, with menarche later. In boys it starts with testicular enlargement.

questions.stem: An 18-month-old said several words at 14 months but now says none and no longer waves or points; he also makes poor eye contact. Growth is normal. What is the most appropriate interpretation?
options: Normal variation — reassure and recheck in 6 months
Loss of previously acquired skills is a red flag warranting prompt developmental evaluation
Expected, because boys talk later than girls
Likely a hearing problem only; no developmental referral needed
questions.explain: Loss of previously acquired skills (regression) at any age is a major developmental red flag — combined with social red flags (no pointing/joint attention, poor eye contact) it warrants prompt evaluation, not watchful waiting.

questions.stem: At a 9-month well-child visit, which skill would be the LEAST expected for a typically developing infant?
questions.explain: Independent walking is usually closer to 12 months (range to ~18 months). At 9 months expect independent sitting, object permanence/peek-a-boo, stranger anxiety, and an emerging pincer grasp.

questions.stem: Parents of an 8-month-old ask about sweetening foods with a little honey. What should you advise?
options: Honey is fine in small amounts from 6 months
Avoid honey until 12 months because of infant botulism risk
Honey is never safe at any age
Honey is only a problem if it is unpasteurized
questions.explain: Avoid honey before 12 months due to the risk of infant botulism (Clostridium botulinum spores).

questions.stem: A 20-month-old drinks roughly 1 L of cow milk daily and eats few solids. He is pale and irritable. CBC: Hb low, MCV low, MCHC low. Which management plan is best?
options: Start folate and recheck in a year
Reduce cow milk to ~500 mL/day, increase iron-rich foods, and start oral iron given with vitamin C
Reassure — a normal ferritin would exclude deficiency even during illness
questions.explain: Excess cow milk displaces iron-rich foods and causes microcytic, hypochromic iron-deficiency anemia. Treat by limiting milk to ~500 mL/day, adding iron-rich foods, and oral iron with vitamin C. Note ferritin can be falsely normal/high as an acute-phase reactant.

questions.stem: Which scenario is a genuine contraindication to breastfeeding?
options: The mother follows a strict vegan diet
There is a strong family history of atopy
The mother has active, untreated tuberculosis
The infant was born at 35 weeks' gestation
questions.explain: Contraindications include active untreated TB, HIV (where safe alternatives exist), certain meds/chemo/radioisotopes, illicit drug exposure, and active herpetic breast lesions. A vegan diet, atopy history, and prematurity are not contraindications (though vegan diet needs nutritional attention, e.g. B12).

questions.stem: A 2-month-old is exclusively breastfed and thriving. Which supplement is routinely recommended?
questions.explain: Vitamin D supplementation is recommended for Canadian infants and is particularly important for exclusively breastfed infants (breast milk is low in vitamin D).

questions.stem: A thriving 5-week-old cries inconsolably for 3–4 hours most evenings over the past 3 weeks. He feeds well, is afebrile, has normal stools, and a normal exam. What is the best management?
options: Start simethicone and a lactase supplement
Reassurance, soothing strategies, validate parental distress, and give return precautions
Order an abdominal ultrasound and stool cultures
questions.explain: This fits infantile colic (Wessel rule of 3; peaks ~6 weeks, resolves by 3–4 months) in a healthy, growing infant. Management is reassurance, support, and safety/return precautions — simethicone, lactase, and sucrose lack good evidence.

questions.stem: A 10-month-old has a beefy-red diaper rash that involves the inguinal folds with small satellite pustules at the edges. What is the best treatment plan?
options: Barrier cream alone — fold involvement rules out yeast
Frequent diaper changes, a barrier cream, and a topical antifungal
A 3-week course of high-potency topical steroid
questions.explain: Fold involvement plus satellite lesions = Candida diaper dermatitis. Treat with frequent changes, barrier protection, and a topical antifungal (clotrimazole/nystatin); a brief low-potency steroid only if very inflamed. Irritant dermatitis instead spares the folds.

questions.stem: Parents describe "breath-holding spells" in their 2-year-old. On closer history, the episodes occur out of the blue with no trigger, sometimes during sleep, and are followed by 10–15 minutes of confusion and drowsiness. There is a family history of sudden unexplained death. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure — these are classic breath-holding spells; check iron status
These features are atypical; pursue evaluation for an alternate cause such as a seizure or cardiac arrhythmia
Advise the parents to ignore the episodes entirely
questions.explain: Classic breath-holding spells have a clear trigger (frustration/pain), are brief, and show rapid return to baseline. No trigger, events during sleep, a prolonged postictal state, and a family history of sudden death are red flags pointing to seizure or a cardiac/arrhythmic cause — investigate rather than reassure.

questions.stem: A child's frontal chest radiograph shows an air-space opacity that obscures the right heart border, but the right hemidiaphragm remains sharply seen. Which lobe is most likely involved?
questions.explain: The silhouette sign: loss of the right heart border = right middle lobe; a preserved hemidiaphragm argues against lower-lobe disease (which would erase the diaphragm). Loss of the left heart border indicates the lingula.

questions.stem: A 10-day-old has noisy, laboured breathing and sweating with feeds. CXR shows a large right posterior mediastinal mass with calcifications compressing the trachea; biopsy reveals a small round blue cell tumour. Which combination of test and prognostic marker is most relevant?
options: Serum AFP and beta-hCG; tumour is a germ-cell tumour
Urinary VMA/HVA and MYCN amplification by FISH; tumour is a neuroblastoma
Urinalysis for hematuria and WT1; tumour is a Wilms tumour
Spirometry and IgE; the mass is reactive thymus
questions.explain: A posterior mediastinal mass with calcifications in a neonate plus SRBCT histology strongly suggests neuroblastoma (neural-crest/sympathetic origin). Support with urinary catecholamine metabolites (VMA/HVA) and obtain MYCN amplification by FISH — an adverse prognostic marker.

cards.prompt: Match each Canadian preventive-care tool to its population:
cards.explain: Rourke for the early years, Greig for 6–17, HEEADSSS for the adolescent interview.

cards.prompt: What does the ITHELLPS mnemonic screen for?
cards.answer: Social determinants of health: Income, Transportation, Housing, Education, Legal status, Literacy, Personal safety, Support.

cards.text: Safe sleep: place infants on their {{back}}, on a {{firm flat}} surface, with {{no}} soft bedding/pillows/bumpers, and {{avoid}} co-sleeping.
cards.explain: "Back to sleep" on a firm flat surface, alone, reduces SIDS and asphyxiation.

cards.prompt: Give two reasons routine infant vaccines require multiple doses.
cards.answer: Initial priming may not be durable, infant immune responses mature over time, maternal antibody can interfere with early responses, and boosters strengthen memory — any two.

cards.prompt: Which are evidence-based strategies to reduce vaccine pain in infants? (select all)
options: Giving the most painful vaccine first
cards.explain: Feed, sweeten, hold upright, distract — and give the most painful vaccine last, not first.

cards.prompt: Which are NOT contraindications to routine childhood vaccination? (select all)
cards.explain: Mild illness, breastfeeding, prematurity, and family allergy history are fine. Severe immunocompromise (and pregnancy) is a true contraindication to live vaccines.

cards.prompt: What does HEEADSSS stand for?
cards.answer: Home, Education/employment, Eating/body image, Activities, Drugs, Sexuality, Suicide/depression, Safety.

cards.prompt: Name situations that break adolescent confidentiality.
cards.answer: Risk of harm to self or others, abuse, and inability to consent / serious safety concerns. Confidentiality is offered but with these explicit limits.

cards.text: Mid-parental height — Boy: [(maternal + {{13}} cm) + paternal] / 2. Girl: [maternal + (paternal − {{13}} cm)] / 2.
cards.explain: Add 13 cm for boys, subtract 13 cm from the father's height for girls.

cards.prompt: Match the short-stature pattern to its hallmark:
pairs: Short parents, normal velocity, normal bone age
Delayed bone age, normal velocity, late-bloomer family
Poor velocity, crossing percentiles, systemic signs
cards.explain: Velocity and bone age separate the benign variants from pathology.

cards.prompt: Which is the dominant driver of intrauterine growth — growth hormone or insulin?
cards.answer: Insulin (with maternal/placental/fetal factors). Growth hormone is NOT the main intrauterine driver — it matters more postnatally.

cards.text: In infancy the dominant driver of growth is {{nutrition}}; in childhood and adolescence {{genetic and hormonal}} influences become increasingly important.
cards.explain: Nutrition first, then genes/hormones take over.

cards.prompt: Put the sequence of puberty in BOYS in order:
cards.explain: Boys: testes → penis → pubic hair → growth spurt.

cards.prompt: Put the sequence of puberty in GIRLS in order:
cards.explain: Girls: thelarche → pubic hair → growth spurt → menarche (late).

cards.prompt: Match age to a hallmark milestone:
pairs: Pretend play; key developmental review visit
cards.explain: Anchor milestones — remember ranges, not exact days.

cards.prompt: Which are developmental red flags? (select all)
cards.explain: Regression, not sitting by 9 mo, no words by 16–18 mo, and no two-word phrases by 2 y are red flags. Walking has a range to ~18 months, so not walking at 13 months alone is not a red flag.

cards.prompt: Why is loss of acquired skills always concerning?
cards.answer: Regression at any age suggests a neurodegenerative, metabolic, or other serious pathology and warrants prompt evaluation — unlike a single borderline milestone, which may be normal variation.

cards.text: Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended for about the first {{6}} months, then continued with complementary foods. Avoid honey before {{12}} months due to botulism risk.
cards.explain: 6 months exclusive; no honey under 12 months.

cards.prompt: Which are contraindications to breastfeeding? (select all)
cards.explain: TB (untreated), active herpetic breast lesions, and HIV (where formula is safe) contraindicate; a vegan diet does not (needs B12 attention).

cards.prompt: Which infants especially need vitamin D supplementation?
cards.answer: All Canadian infants are recommended supplementation, but exclusively/predominantly breastfed infants especially, because breast milk is low in vitamin D.

cards.text: Limit toddler cow milk to about {{500}} mL/day to prevent {{iron-deficiency}} anemia. Use {{whole}} milk until age 2, then lower-fat.
cards.explain: Excess milk displaces iron-rich foods and causes IDA.

cards.prompt: When solids are introduced around 6 months, which foods should be prioritized?
options: Cow milk as the main drink
cards.explain: Prioritize iron-rich foods at solid introduction; avoid juice, excess cow milk, and honey under 12 months.

cards.text: Iron-deficiency anemia shows {{microcytic}} {{hypochromic}} RBCs. Ferritin can be falsely {{elevated}} during inflammation because it is an acute-phase reactant.
cards.explain: Low MCV/MCHC; interpret ferritin in clinical context.

cards.prompt: Why give oral iron with vitamin C, and what safety point must parents hear?
cards.answer: Vitamin C enhances iron absorption. Safety: iron causes dark stools/constipation and is dangerous in overdose — keep it out of children's reach.

cards.prompt: State the "division of responsibility" for picky eating.
cards.answer: The parent decides what, when, and where food is offered; the child decides whether and how much to eat. No pressure or punishment; repeated exposure.

cards.text: Colic — Wessel rule of 3: crying ≥{{3}} hours/day, ≥{{3}} days/week, for ≥{{3}} weeks; peaks around {{6}} weeks and resolves by 3–4 months.
cards.explain: A clinical pattern in a healthy, growing infant.

cards.prompt: Which are red flags that argue AGAINST simple colic? (select all)
cards.explain: Bilious emesis, blood in stool, poor weight gain, lethargy, fever, or a hair tourniquet point to another diagnosis. Evening crying with normal growth is typical colic.

cards.prompt: Match each feature to physiologic GER or GERD:
pairs: Poor weight gain, feeding refusal, hematemesis
cards.explain: Growth and pain/red-flags separate GER from GERD.

cards.prompt: A diaper rash that spares the skin folds and affects convex surfaces is most likely:
cards.explain: Irritant dermatitis hits convex surfaces and spares folds; Candida involves the folds with satellite lesions.

cards.prompt: What two features distinguish Candida diaper dermatitis, and what is the treatment?
cards.answer: Involvement of the skin folds plus satellite lesions. Treat with barrier care plus a topical antifungal (clotrimazole/nystatin).

cards.prompt: Which deficiency is associated with breath-holding spells, and why check it?
cards.answer: Iron deficiency — it is associated with breath-holding spells, and treating iron deficiency can reduce the frequency of events.

cards.prompt: Which features should prompt evaluation for an alternative to classic breath-holding spells? (select all)
options: Brief cyanosis then rapid recovery after a tantrum
cards.explain: No trigger, events during sleep/exertion, prolonged altered state, and family history of sudden death are red flags. Quick recovery after a tantrum trigger is the classic, reassuring pattern.

cards.prompt: Why is physical punishment (spanking) not recommended?
cards.answer: It can worsen aggression, damage the parent-child relationship/trust, and does not teach self-regulation. Use calm, consistent limits and positive reinforcement instead.

cards.text: Night terrors arise from {{non-REM}} sleep; management is {{reassurance}} and safety — do not wake the child.
cards.explain: Parasomnia from deep non-REM sleep; benign and self-limited.

cards.prompt: What does ALARA mean and why does it matter more in children?
cards.answer: As Low As Reasonably Achievable — minimize radiation dose. Children are more radiosensitive and have more years for radiation-associated malignancy to develop.

cards.prompt: Which imaging modalities avoid ionizing radiation? (select all)
cards.explain: US and MRI use no ionizing radiation; radiographs are low-dose and CT is the highest.

cards.prompt: Match the lost CXR border to the location of disease:
cards.explain: The silhouette sign localizes opacities by which adjacent border is effaced.

cards.text: On CXR, the endotracheal tube tip should sit {{above the carina}}, and an enteric tube tip/side-port should be in the {{stomach}} after crossing the diaphragm.
cards.explain: Standard line/tube checks.

cards.prompt: Order the density scale on a radiograph from most lucent (black) to most opaque (white):
cards.explain: Air is black; metal is brightest white — increasing density increases opacity.

cards.prompt: Name three categories in the pediatric small round blue cell tumour differential.
cards.answer: Lymphoma, sarcomas (e.g. rhabdomyosarcoma, Ewing), and blastomas (e.g. neuroblastoma, Wilms, retinoblastoma). Distinguished by site, IHC, and molecular/cytogenetic tests.

cards.prompt: From which tissue does neuroblastoma arise, and what are its two most common sites?
cards.answer: Neural-crest / sympathetic nervous system precursors. Common sites: the adrenal gland and the sympathetic chain (including the posterior mediastinum).

cards.text: Neuroblastoma workup: urinary catecholamine metabolites {{VMA}} and {{HVA}}; the key adverse prognostic marker is {{MYCN}} amplification, assessed by {{FISH}}.
cards.explain: Catecholamine metabolites support the diagnosis; MYCN amplification is adverse.

cards.prompt: For a suspected neuroblastoma, why is FNA alone often inadequate?
options: FNA is too painful in children
Diagnosis and molecular testing need architecture and adequate tissue (core or open biopsy)
FNA cannot reach the adrenal gland
Catecholamines must be measured in tissue
cards.explain: Core needle or open incisional biopsy is preferred so there is enough tissue for histologic architecture and ancillary/molecular testing (e.g. MYCN).

cards.prompt: Which factors worsen neuroblastoma prognosis? (select all)
cards.explain: MYCN amplification, segmental chromosomal changes, older age, and higher stage are adverse; better differentiation is favourable.

cards.prompt: Which malignancies dominate under age 10 vs over age 10?
cards.answer: Under 10: leukemias, blastomas (retinoblastoma, neuroblastoma, Wilms, hepatoblastoma), brain tumours. Over 10: lymphoma, sarcomas (osteosarcoma, Ewing), and some carcinomas.

guides.title: Week 09 · 1 Well-Child, Vaccines & Adolescent Care
guides.html:
Preventive Pediatrics

Well-Child, Vaccines & Adolescent Care

Week 09 · Pediatrics
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

The preventive backbone of Week 9: well-child surveillance tools, safe-sleep and injury counselling, the immunization rationale and hesitancy script, and the confidential adolescent visit.

Well-child visits

Preventive encounters covering growth surveillance, developmental screening, immunizations, nutrition, physical exam, injury prevention, family functioning, and anticipatory guidance.

ToolPopulation
Rourke Baby RecordCanadian standard for infants / young children
Greig Health RecordChildren & adolescents 6–17 y
Every visit is a chance to screen social determinants of health. Mnemonic ITHELLPS — Income, Transportation, Housing, Education, Legal status, Literacy, Personal safety, Support.

Safety & anticipatory guidance

Do-not-miss — safe sleep
Back to sleep · firm flat surface · no soft bedding/pillows/toys · avoid co-sleeping · avoid smoke exposure. Reduces SIDS and asphyxiation.
  • Car seats chosen by developmental stage/size; correct restraint cuts mortality and injury severity.
  • Injury focus evolves with age: falls/burns/choking/poisoning in infants/toddlers → helmets, water/road safety, substance & sexual safety, violence prevention in older children.
  • OTC cough/cold meds are generally not recommended in young children — supportive care + return precautions.

Childhood immunizations

Routine schedule prevents diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis, polio, Hib, pneumococcus, rotavirus, measles, mumps, rubella, varicella, meningococcus, HPV, influenza, and more (varies by age/province).

Why multiple doses?

Initial priming may not be durable, infant immune responses mature over time, maternal antibody can interfere, and boosters strengthen memory responses.

Side effects & pain reduction

  • Common: local pain, redness/swelling, low-grade fever, irritability, sleepiness. Anaphylaxis is rare but must be anticipated.
  • Pain: breast/bottle feed during injection, oral sucrose/dextrose for infants, upright cuddling, topical anesthetic, distraction, and give the most painful vaccine last.
Hesitancy counselling
Open with a presumptive but respectful recommendation ("Today she is due for these vaccines"). Use motivational interviewing: ask permission, explore the specific concern, affirm the parent's goal, give targeted (not overwhelming) information. Contrast disease risk vs vaccine risk; explain community protection and the danger of delaying infant protection.
NOT contraindications to routine vaccines
Mild illness, breastfeeding, family history of allergy, prematurity. Live vaccines are the ones to withhold in severe immunocompromise/pregnancy.

Adolescent care

Ask the caregiver to step out for part of the visit; explain confidentiality and its limits (harm to self/others, abuse, inability to consent) before the psychosocial history.

HEEADSSS — Home, Education/employment, Eating/body image, Activities/peers, Drugs, Sexuality (relationships/contraception/STIs), Suicide/depression/anxiety, Safety/violence.
  • Counsel ≥60 min/day moderate-to-vigorous activity; screen-time guidance now emphasizes function/sleep displacement/cyberbullying over strict counting.
  • Harm reduction: seatbelts/helmets, substance safety, consent, STI prevention, contraception, mental-health resources, trusted-adult support.

guides.title: Week 09 · 2 Growth, Puberty & Development
guides.html:
Surveillance

Growth, Puberty & Development

Week 09 · Pediatrics
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

How to read a growth curve, calculate mid-parental height, separate the three short-stature patterns, sequence puberty, and recognize developmental red flags.

Normal growth principles

Growth reflects general health — shaped by genetics, nutrition, environment, chronic disease, medications, and hormones.

  • Intrauterine: driven by maternal/placental/fetal factors and insulin — growth hormone is not the main intrauterine driver.
  • Infancy: nutrition dominates. Childhood/adolescence: genetic & hormonal influences rise.
  • Assess with serial measurements, correct plotting, growth velocity, pubertal stage, mid-parental height, body proportions.
Trajectory > single point. Short stature with poor growth velocity is pathologic until proven otherwise.

Mid-parental height

ChildFormula
Boy[(maternal ht + 13 cm) + paternal ht] / 2
Girl[maternal ht + (paternal ht − 13 cm)] / 2

Short-stature patterns

PatternDiagnosisKey features
Short parents, normal velocity, normal bone ageFamilial short statureProjected height matches family; no systemic symptoms
Delayed puberty, delayed bone age, normal velocity, family of late bloomersConstitutional delayFinal height usually normal; more common in boys
Poor velocity, crossing percentiles, systemic symptoms, abnormal proportionsPathologicChronic/endocrine disease, syndromes, malnutrition
DisproportionateSkeletal dysplasia / syndromeCheck upper/lower segment ratio, arm span, dysmorphism

Puberty

Sequence — memorize
Boys (begin 9–14 y): testicular enlargement → penile growth → pubic hair → growth spurt.
Girls (begin 8–13 y): breast development (thelarche) first → pubic hair → growth spurt → menarche later.

Interpret precocious/delayed puberty against growth velocity, bone age, neurologic symptoms, chronic disease, and family pattern.

Development

Domains

Gross motor · fine motor/adaptive · speech/language · cognitive/problem-solving · social-emotional. The 18-month visit is a key developmental review point.

AgeGross motorFine/cognitiveLanguage/social
2 moLifts headOpens handsSocial smile; coos
4 moHead control; rolls front→backHands to midlineLaughs; turns to voice
6 moTripod sitTransfers; raking graspBabbles
9 moSits independently; crawlsImmature pincer; object permanencePeek-a-boo; stranger anxiety; responds to name
12 moPulls to stand; cruises; may stepPincer; points1–2 words; follows 1-step command
18 moWalks/runs stifflyScribbles; uses spoonSeveral words; pretend play
2 yRuns, kicks ballStacks blocksTwo-word phrases
3–4 yTricycle; stairs alternatingCopies circle/crossSentences; imaginative play
Developmental red flags
  • Loss of previously acquired skills at any age (regression).
  • No social smile by ~2–3 mo; poor eye contact / no joint attention.
  • Not sitting by ~9 mo; not walking by ~18 mo.
  • No words by ~16–18 mo; no two-word phrases by ~2 y.
  • Major asymmetry, persistent primitive reflexes, abnormal tone, multi-domain delay.

guides.title: Week 09 · 3 Infant & Child Nutrition
guides.html:
Feeding

Infant & Child Nutrition

Week 09 · Pediatrics
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Breastfeeding benefits and true contraindications, vitamin D / iron / solids timing, the honey rule, the toddler milk limit, and the iron-deficiency-anemia story.

Breast/chest feeding & formula

Exclusive breast/chest feeding for ~first 6 months, then continued with complementary foods beyond 6 mo as feasible.

  • Infant benefits: optimal nutrition; protection vs otitis media/gastroenteritis/RSV; lower SIDS risk; allergy/asthma & obesity benefit; safe and economical.
  • Parent benefits: bonding, delayed menses, reduced breast/ovarian cancer risk, weight management.
Breastfeeding contraindications
Untreated active TB, HIV (where safe alternatives exist), certain meds/chemo/radioisotopes, illicit drug exposure, active herpetic breast lesions.
NOT contraindications: vegan diet or family history of atopy (but need nutritional attention).

Formula: standard cow-milk-based for most; extensively hydrolyzed / amino-acid for cow-milk-protein allergy; soy only for selected indications; specialized formulas for prematurity/metabolic disease.

Vitamin D, iron, solids, milk

  • Vitamin D supplementation recommended for Canadian infants — especially breastfed infants (follow local dosing).
  • Introduce complementary solids ~6 mo when developmentally ready; prioritize iron-rich foods (meats, fish, poultry, eggs, legumes, iron-fortified cereal, tofu).
  • Introduce common allergenic foods in safe forms once solids start; avoid whole nuts/choking forms.
  • Avoid honey before 12 months — infant botulism risk.
Toddler cow milk ≤ ~500 mL/day (~16 oz). Excess milk → iron-deficiency anemia and displaces iron-rich foods. Whole milk to age 2, then lower-fat is typical.

Picky eating — division of responsibility

Parent decides what/when/where; child decides whether/how much. Keep routine, repeated exposure, no pressure/punishment; avoid grazing and excessive milk/juice.

Iron-deficiency anemia

  • Classic: toddler on excessive cow milk + limited solids; pallor, fatigue, irritability, pica, poor feeding, developmental concern.
  • Labs: microcytic hypochromic anemia; smear small pale RBCs / pencil cells. Ferritin can be falsely elevated by inflammation (acute-phase reactant) — interpret in context; consider iron studies + reticulocytes.
  • Management: reduce milk, increase iron-rich foods, oral iron if deficient, give with vitamin C to boost absorption; counsel re dark stools/constipation and keep iron out of reach (overdose is dangerous).

guides.title: Week 09 · 4 Common Pediatric Problems
guides.html:
Outpatient

Common Pediatric Problems

Week 09 · Pediatrics
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

Colic, GER vs GERD, the diaper-rash triad, breath-holding spells and their iron link, and the behavioural basics (tantrums, discipline, toilet training, parasomnias).

Infantile colic

Excessive crying without clear cause in an otherwise healthy, growing infant. Wessel rule of 3: ≥3 h/day, ≥3 days/week, ≥3 weeks; onset <3 mo. Peaks ~6 weeks, resolves by 3–4 mo.

Exclude red flags first
Fever, poor feeding, bilious emesis, blood in stool, lethargy, abnormal exam, poor weight gain, injury, hair tourniquet, corneal abrasion.

Management: reassurance, validate parental distress, soothing strategies, feeding technique, respite/safety plan. No good evidence for simethicone, lactase, or sucrose; probiotic evidence is mixed.

GER vs GERD

FeaturePhysiologic GERGERD / concerning
GrowthNormalPoor weight gain / FTT
SymptomsEffortless spit-ups, "happy spitter"Pain/feeding refusal, hematemesis, apnea/cyanosis, recurrent pneumonia, severe irritability
ManagementReassure, avoid overfeeding, burp/upright after feedsAssess red flags; cow-milk-protein-free trial if formula-fed; targeted Ix/referral if severe/refractory

Diaper dermatitis

TypeCluesManagement
IrritantErythema on convex surfaces; spares foldsFrequent changes, gentle cleansing, air, thick zinc-oxide/petrolatum barrier
CandidaBeefy red, involves folds + satellite lesionsBarrier + topical antifungal (clotrimazole/nystatin); brief low-potency steroid only if very inflamed
Bacterial/otherPustules, erosions, honey crust, systemic/refractoryConsider bacterial infection, psoriasis, seborrhea, LCH; treat/refer

Breath-holding spells

Toddler/preschool; triggered by anger/frustration or pain. Child cries → holds breath → cyanotic or pallid → brief LOC → rapid return to baseline.

Reassure if classic; check iron status (iron deficiency is associated; treating reduces events). Keep child safe; avoid reinforcing the behaviour.
Red flags for an alternate diagnosis
Events during exertion or sleep, prolonged postictal state, no trigger, family history of sudden death, abnormal cardiac/neuro exam.

Tantrums, discipline, toilet training, sleep

  • Tantrums are developmentally normal in toddlers: calm/consistent response, positive reinforcement, redirection, ignore minor attention-seeking when safe, meet sleep/hunger needs.
  • Spanking/physical punishment is not recommended — worsens aggression, damages trust, doesn't teach self-regulation.
  • Toilet training when ready (often 18 mo–3 y): stays dry longer, interest, follows simple instructions, communicates needs. Positive reinforcement, no punishment/pressure.
  • Sleep / parasomnias: night terrors arise from non-REM sleep — reassurance & safety, don't wake the child.

guides.title: Week 09 · 5 Pediatric Imaging & CXR
guides.html:
Radiology

Pediatric Imaging & CXR

Week 09 · Pediatrics
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

ALARA and modality choice in children, the outside-in CXR read, the silhouette sign for localizing opacities, and correct line/tube tip positions.

Radiation risk & modality choice

Children have higher lifetime sensitivity to ionizing radiation and more years for malignancy to manifest. Use ALARA (as low as reasonably achievable).

ModalityRadiationUse
US, MRINonePreferred when adequate
RadiographLowFirst-line for many questions
CTHighHigh detail; must be justified — will it change management?
Counsel with balanced language: image when benefits outweigh risks and results will change management.

CXR approach

Outside-in: identifying data/quality → soft tissues → upper abdomen → bones → heart/mediastinum → pleura → airways → lungs.

Density scale: air = lucent/black; soft tissue / fluid / bone / metal are increasingly opaque/white.

Silhouette sign — localizing

Lost borderLocation
Right heart borderRight middle lobe
Left heart borderLingula
HemidiaphragmLower lobe

The lateral film aids anterior/posterior localization. Infection/aspiration appears as consolidation/air-space opacity; aspiration favours dependent regions by position.

Lines & tubes

DeviceCorrect tip
ETTAbove the carina
Enteric tubeCrosses diaphragm; side-port/tip in stomach
Central lineAppropriate central venous position (not arterial, not too deep)

guides.title: Week 09 · 6 Tumours & Neuroblastoma
guides.html:
Oncology / Path

Pediatric Tumours & Neuroblastoma

Week 09 · Pediatrics
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite

The small-round-blue-cell-tumour differential by age, and neuroblastoma end-to-end: origin, sites, age-based presentation, VMA/HVA, MYCN/FISH prognosis, and biopsy strategy.

Small round blue cell tumours (SRBCT)

Pediatric malignancies differ by age:

  • <10 y: leukemias, blastomas (retinoblastoma, neuroblastoma, nephroblastoma/Wilms, hepatoblastoma), brain tumours.
  • >10 y: lymphoma, sarcomas (osteosarcoma, Ewing), some carcinomas.

SRBCTs look similar on H&E. Differential by site: lymphoma, sarcomas (rhabdomyosarcoma, Ewing), blastomas (neuroblastoma, Wilms, retinoblastoma).

Pathologists distinguish SRBCTs using site of origin, morphologic differentiation, immunohistochemistry, cytogenetics/molecular tests, and ancillary studies.

Neuroblastoma

Peripheral neuroblastic tumour from neural-crest / sympathetic precursors. Common sites: adrenal gland and sympathetic chain (including posterior mediastinum).

Presentation by age

  • Neonate/young infant: congenital mass, respiratory symptoms from thoracic mass, hepatomegaly/skin nodules.
  • Older child: abdominal mass, pain, systemic symptoms, bone pain, metastatic disease.
Workup
Biochemistry: urinary catecholamine metabolites VMA / HVA. Imaging/staging: local extent, critical structures, resectability, metastases, image-defined risk factors. Biopsy must provide enough tissue for diagnosis and molecular testing.
Prognosis
Age/stage, histology/differentiation, mitosis-karyorrhexis index, MYCN amplification (adverse; assessed by FISH), ploidy, segmental chromosomal abnormalities.
For suspected neuroblastoma, prefer core needle / open incisional biopsy over FNA alone when architecture & ancillary testing are needed.

Week 9 case anchor

A 7-day-old with laboured breathing and sweating with feeds — CXR shows a large right posterior mediastinal opacity with mass effect on trachea/right main bronchus and calcific foci; CT shows a heterogeneous enhancing posterior mediastinal soft-tissue mass. Differential: lymphoma, neurogenic tumour, metastasis. Tumour-board questions: What is it? Does it need to come out? Is it resectable?

guides.title: Week 09 · 7 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

Pediatrics

Week 09 · Pediatrics
CPC 1 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Febrile infant <90 days → full sepsis workup + empiric antibiotics
Fever + non-blanching purpura → meningococcemia — urgent antibiotics
Bilious vomiting in a neonate → malrotation / volvulus — surgical emergency
Currant-jelly stool + colicky pain (intussusception) → ultrasound → air enema
Abdominal mass in a child → neuroblastoma / Wilms — imaging + referral (don't keep palpating Wilms)
Non-accidental injury red flags → protect the child + report

Well-child & vaccines Preventive

Schedule routine immunizations at set ages; catch-up if behind
Live vaccines avoid in significant immunocompromise / pregnancy
Newborn screen metabolic heel-prick + hearing
Surveillance developmental screening at well-child visits
Adolescents confidential care; HEADSS psychosocial screen

Growth & development Growth

Plot it weight/length/head circumference on charts; track the TREND
Milestones gross/fine motor, language, social — know red-flag delays
Short stature constitutional/familial (benign) vs pathologic (cross percentiles)
Head size macro-/microcephaly → investigate
Puberty Tanner staging; work up precocious or delayed

Nutrition Feeding

Infancy breastfeeding preferred; vitamin D supplementation
Solids introduce ~6 months; iron-rich foods
Avoid honey <1 yr (botulism); cow's milk as main drink <1 yr
Failure to thrive intake vs losses vs medical; plot weight
Iron deficiency excess cow's milk; screen toddlers

Common problems Acute

Acute otitis media amoxicillin first-line (or watchful waiting)
Bronchiolitis supportive (RSV); no routine bronchodilators/steroids
Croup barky cough, stridor → dexamethasone
Febrile seizure usually benign 6 mo–5 yr; reassure
Constipation very common; disimpaction + maintenance
Viral exanthems supportive; recognize the classics

Imaging Radiology

Principle minimize radiation (ALARA); ultrasound first-line for many
CXR a normal thymus can mimic a mediastinal mass
Intussusception target sign on US → air enema (also therapeutic)
Pyloric stenosis hypertrophic pylorus; projectile non-bilious vomiting

Tumours Oncology

Neuroblastoma adrenal/sympathetic chain; catecholamines; periorbital ecchymoses
Wilms tumour painless abdominal mass; avoid vigorous palpation
Leukemia (ALL) most common childhood cancer; cytopenias + blasts
Retinoblastoma leukocoria (white reflex)
Brain tumours posterior fossa; morning headache + vomiting
Week 09 · Pediatrics · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.adverse: GI upset, renal; avoid in dehydration / <6 months
Generally well tolerated as a single dose

facets.use: Wheeze / reactive airways (not routine in bronchiolitis)
Routine newborn prophylaxis (prevents hemorrhagic disease of the newborn)
facets.mechanism: Cofactor for clotting factors II, VII, IX, X
